method: QE ph.x DFPT, 4x4x4 q-grid (21 irreducible q), electron_phonon='simple'

falsifier_results.name: Gamma-point dynamical stability (all real phonon modes)
falsifier_results.evidence: cabeh8.dyn1 (Gamma): 8 of 20 modes imaginary; largest freq(1)=-329.191 THz (-10980.6 cm-1), freq(2)=-328.717 THz, freq(3)=-183.124 THz. Acoustic-sum-rule residuals at Gamma are normally a few cm-1; these are 1e4 cm-1 imaginary => saddle-point / violently unstable structure.

falsifier_results.name: Full q-grid dynamical stability (no imaginary modes off-Gamma)
falsifier_results.evidence: Imaginary modes pervasive across grid: dyn1=8, dyn10=8, dyn15=10, dyn21=10 negative-freq modes (of 20). Instability is not a localized Gamma artifact.

falsifier_results.name: Physical el-ph coupling (lambda >= 0 per mode)
falsifier_results.evidence: Per-mode lambda from ph.out negative on the soft modes: lambda(5)=-0.9434, lambda(6)=-0.5380, lambda(1)=-0.1427. Negative lambda is the el-ph signature of imaginary phonons => Eliashberg/Allen-Dynes Tc is undefined; the structure cannot host phonon-mediated superconductivity at this geometry/pressure.

scope_caveats: This is a VALID closed-negative result (d_paper_negative_ok), analogous to CaAuH3. It rules out CaBeH8 at this pressure (~50 GPa, the deck's vc-relax press) as a phonon-mediated RTSC candidate in its current relaxed geometry.

The original el-ph run also crashed at q14 with an MPI segfault (likely _ph0 scratch-disk exhaustion on a full overlay). That crash is NOT the finding -- the finding is the dynamical instability, which is independent of and prior to the disk crash. Re-running the full grid with disk headroom would only re-compute unphysical coupling on imaginary modes.
Does NOT rule out a DIFFERENT CaBeH8 polymorph / pressure. A higher-pressure phase or alternate symmetry could be dynamically stable -- that would be a separate deck.
breakthrough_paths: Re-relax CaBeH8 at substantially higher pressure (e.g. 100-200 GPa) -- hydride clathrates frequently stabilize only above a threshold pressure; the soft modes may harden.
Symmetry-lower / distort along the dominant imaginary eigenvector (freq(1) at Gamma) and re-relax -- the structure may relax into a stable lower-symmetry well.
SSCHA (anharmonic) treatment -- quantum/anharmonic effects can dynamically stabilize hydrides whose harmonic phonons are imaginary; this is the standard rescue for soft H-modes.
